behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type)
behaviors.justification: The real failure is when the LLM generates a function with wrong parameter names, types, or return type. Mocking the LLM to always return correct signatures hides this failure mode. Parsing/validation logic (testable at Unit/Integration) is necessary but insufficient — we must verify the LLM actually respects the interface specification. A real LLM call is required to detect signature hallucinations like `def foo(x)` instead of `def transform(data: str, config: dict) -> list`.
behaviors.test_description: A real LLM is given a function specification requiring specific parameter names and types. The system extracts the signature from generated code and validates it matches the spec. Test passes only if mismatches (wrong params, wrong types, wrong return type) are correctly identified or generation is retried.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on first or subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: The real failure: 'the LLM-generated code is functionally incorrect and fails the test suite.' This requires executing the generated code against real tests—mocking test execution defeats the purpose. The core variable is whether the LLM produces working code (not simulator behavior). We need real LLM output + real test execution (subprocess) to detect failures like off-by-one errors, wrong algorithm choice, or type mismatches that only manifest at runtime.
behaviors.test_description: A real LLM generates code for a function. The system executes the generated code in a subprocess against a provided test suite. Test passes only if all tests pass (or refinement succeeds within 3 attempts). Test fails if the code crashes, hangs, or produces wrong results.

behaviors.description: Failure messages are included in the refinement prompt so the model can learn from mistakes
behaviors.justification: The real failure: 'the system fails to extract or pass test failure messages to the LLM in the refinement prompt.' This is a data-flow and prompt-construction issue, not an LLM capability issue. We can mock the LLM and inspect the prompts sent to it to verify failure messages are correctly extracted, formatted, and included. Mocking will not hide failures here—we can intercept and assert on the prompt content. A real LLM is not needed to test the feedback mechanism itself.
behaviors.test_description: The system generates code (real or mocked), runs test suite, and generates a second prompt when tests fail. With a mocked LLM, the test intercepts the second call and asserts the prompt includes the test failure output (stderr, assertion errors, etc.) in a clear, parseable format.

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns structured error with all attempts and results
behaviors.justification: The real failure: 'the system does not enforce the 3-attempt limit or returns incomplete/malformed error structure.' This is a retry-loop and error-construction issue, testable without real LLM calls. Mock the LLM to always generate failing code, verify the loop runs exactly 3 times, and validate the error response structure (contains all 3 attempts with their test results). Mocking will not hide failures in retry logic or error serialization.
behaviors.test_description: With a mocked LLM that always generates code that fails the test suite, the system is invoked. Test verifies: (1) the LLM is called exactly 3 times, (2) after 3 failures, the system returns a structured error (not an exception), (3) the error includes all 3 attempts with their test results and failure messages.

behaviors.description: Full iterative loop (generate, test, refine, test again) produces working implementation for a function a single LLM call typically gets wrong
behaviors.justification: The real failure: 'the LLM cannot improve code through iterative feedback' or 'the feedback loop breaks the LLM's ability to self-correct.' This requires multiple chained agentic steps: LLM call → test → failure analysis → LLM call with feedback → test again. Each step depends on the previous result. Mocking the LLM (e.g., 'first call fails, second call succeeds') tests the harness, not the real behavior. The core claim is that feedback helps the LLM improve—this can only be validated with real LLM calls demonstrating actual learning. This is a full agent pipeline, not a single LLM call.
behaviors.test_description: Real LLM is given a function that single-call generation typically fails on (e.g., complex recursion, subtle edge case). System runs the full loop: generate → test → if failed, extract errors and call LLM again with feedback → test again. Test passes only if code passes tests on attempt 2 or 3. Test verifies the feedback in each prompt is distinct and progressively helpful. Failure: LLM never improves, or feedback loop degrades code quality.
